name: Boxing Stance and Guard
instructions: Stand with feet about shoulder-width apart, lead foot forward and rear foot back at roughly a 45-degree angle (left foot lead for orthodox, right for southpaw).
Bend your knees slightly, keep weight balanced over the balls of both feet, and stay light enough to move in any direction.
Tuck your chin down, raise both hands to cheek level, and keep your elbows in to protect your ribs.
Hold this relaxed, mobile guard as your home base to return to after every movement.
modifications: Practice in front of a mirror to check alignment.
Hold for shorter sets and rest if your legs fatigue.
cautions: Stay relaxed; tensing the shoulders for long periods can strain the neck and traps.
This is informational fundamentals, not a substitute for a qualified coach.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references (e.g. Legends Boxing, Sting Sports beginner guides). Described in own words.

name: Boxing Footwork (Step-and-Slide)
instructions: From your boxing stance, move by stepping with the foot nearest your direction of travel first, then sliding the other foot to restore your stance width.
Step forward with the lead foot to advance, back with the rear foot to retreat, and in the matching direction to move laterally.
Keep your feet under you the whole time so you never cross them or get caught flat.
Stay on the balls of your feet and keep your guard up while you move.
modifications: Move slowly in a small box pattern before adding speed.
Practice each direction separately first.
cautions: Keep knees soft to protect them when changing direction.
Avoid quick pivots on a slippery floor.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Jab
instructions: From your guard, extend your lead hand straight toward the target, turning the fist so the knuckles finish roughly palm-down.
Rotate your lead shoulder slightly and keep the rear hand up to guard your chin.
Snap the punch out and retract it back to guard along the same line immediately.
Exhale sharply on the punch and keep your feet planted.
modifications: Throw at half speed to groove the line before adding power.
Practice the retraction alone so the hand always returns to guard.
cautions: Warm up the shoulders and wrists first.
Do not lock out the elbow violently; extend with control to protect the joint.
Keep the wrist straight on contact to avoid sprains; if you feel pain, stop.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Cross (Straight Rear Hand)
instructions: From your guard, drive off the ball of your rear foot and rotate your rear hip and shoulder toward the target.
Extend the rear hand straight along your centerline, finishing with the knuckles roughly palm-down.
Keep your lead hand up to protect your chin and let your body rotation power the punch, not just the arm.
Retract straight back to guard and reset your stance.
modifications: Practice the hip and foot rotation slowly without the arm first.
Throw at reduced power to learn the mechanics.
cautions: Warm up shoulders, hips, and wrists.
Keep the wrist straight on contact to protect it.
Do not over-rotate so far that you lose balance or strain the lower back; stop if you feel pain.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Hook
instructions: From your guard, keep your elbow bent to about 90 degrees and lift it to roughly the height of the target.
Pivot on the foot on the same side as the punching hand and rotate your hips and torso to swing the fist horizontally inward.
Keep the opposite hand up to guard your chin throughout.
Return the hand to guard and reset; practice on both lead and rear sides.
modifications: Practice the pivot and rotation slowly without power.
Keep the arc short and tight rather than wide and looping.
cautions: Warm up first, especially shoulders, wrists, and knees.
The pivot loads the knee; keep it tracking over the foot and stop if it hurts.
Keep the wrist firm and straight on contact to avoid injury.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Uppercut
instructions: From your guard, bend your knees slightly and drop the punching-side shoulder a touch to load.
Drive upward through your legs and rotate your hips as you send the fist in a short vertical arc toward the chin level of an imaginary target, palm facing you.
Keep the punch compact; do not wind up or drop your hand low first.
Return to guard and reset; practice on both sides.
modifications: Practice the leg drive and hip rotation slowly first.
Keep the motion small to protect your balance.
cautions: Warm up first.
Use leg drive rather than yanking with the arm to protect the shoulder.
Keep the wrist firm; stop if you feel joint pain.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Slip (Head Movement)
instructions: From your guard, bend slightly at the knees and waist and rotate your torso to move your head just off the centerline.
Slip to one side to let an imaginary straight punch pass by your head, keeping both hands up.
Move only as far as needed and immediately return to your balanced stance.
Alternate slipping left and right with control.
modifications: Practice small, slow slips before adding speed.
Use a hanging string or doorway as a reference line.
cautions: Move from the legs and torso, not by craning the neck, to protect the cervical spine.
Do not slip so far that you lose balance.
Stop if you feel neck or back pain.
source: General boxing defensive fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Roll / Duck Under (Bob and Weave)
instructions: From your guard, bend your knees to lower your level, keeping your back fairly straight and chin tucked.
Trace a shallow U-shape with your upper body to pass under an imaginary hook, coming up on the other side.
Keep both hands up at your cheeks the entire time.
Rise back to your stance and reset; alternate directions.
modifications: Keep the dip shallow at first.
Hold a light squat to build the leg endurance the movement needs.
cautions: Bend at the knees, not the lower back, to protect your spine.
Do not drop your head so low or fast that you get dizzy.
Warm up legs first; stop if knees or back hurt.
source: General boxing defensive fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Shadowboxing
instructions: Move around in your stance and throw a mix of punches and defensive movements at an imaginary opponent.
Combine footwork, jabs, crosses, hooks, uppercuts, slips, and rolls in a continuous, relaxed flow.
Stay light on your feet, return to guard after every action, and breathe with each punch.
Treat it as a moving warm-up and technique-grooving drill rather than a power workout.
modifications: Go slow and focus on form.
Limit to one or two techniques at a time when learning.
cautions: Warm up first.
Avoid snapping the elbows or knees into full lockout when there is no target to absorb the punch.
Stay relaxed to avoid neck and shoulder strain.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references (shadowboxing workouts). Described in own words.

name: Heavy Bag Combinations
instructions: Wrap your hands and put on bag gloves before touching the bag.
From your stance, throw controlled combinations on the heavy bag, returning to guard after each.
Move around the bag with footwork rather than standing flat in front of it.
Focus on landing with a straight wrist and a firm but relaxed fist; build speed only once form is solid.
modifications: Throw single punches before full combinations.
Hit at lighter power while you learn alignment.
cautions: Always wrap your hands and wear gloves to protect your wrists and knuckles.
Warm up the wrists, elbows, and shoulders first.
Do not punch with a bent wrist; stop at any sharp joint pain.
This is informational fundamentals, not a substitute for a qualified coach.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Focus Mitt Work (Partner)
instructions: One partner wears focus mitts and calls out punches or combinations; the other throws them with proper form.
The puncher wraps hands and wears gloves, returning to guard after each combination.
The mitt holder meets each punch with a firm pad and keeps the targets at realistic heights.
Switch roles partway through so both partners drill.
modifications: Start with single punches and slow calls.
Reduce power until both partners are comfortable with timing.
cautions: Requires a willing, communicating partner; agree on intensity beforehand.
Wrap hands and wear gloves; warm up first.
The mitt holder should brace each pad to protect their own wrists.
Stop immediately if either partner feels pain.
Informational fundamentals only, not a substitute for a coach.
source: General boxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Teep (Push Kick)
instructions: From your stance, lift the knee of the kicking leg up toward your chest to chamber the kick.
Extend the leg straight out and push through the ball of your foot into the target, as if stamping forward.
Keep your hands up and lean back slightly for balance.
Retract the foot back to the chamber, then return it to your stance.
modifications: Practice the knee chamber and balance without the push first.
Hold a wall or chair for balance while learning.
cautions: Warm up hips and hamstrings.
Do not snap the knee into full lockout; push with control to protect the joint.
Point through the ball of the foot, not the toes, to avoid foot injury.
Stop if you feel hip or knee pain.
source: General Muay Thai fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Roundhouse Kick (Muay Thai)
instructions: From your stance, step your lead foot slightly out for room and pivot hard on the ball of your supporting foot.
Swing the kicking leg around in a horizontal arc, turning your hip over so power comes from the rotation of your whole body.
Make contact with your shin, not your foot, keeping your toes pointed.
Keep your hands up and bring the leg back to your stance after the kick; practice both sides.
modifications: Practice the pivot and hip turn slowly without full speed.
Kick at low or medium height while learning.
Hold a chair for balance at first.
cautions: Warm up thoroughly; this kick loads the hip, knee, and ankle.
Pivot fully on the supporting foot so the supporting knee is not twisted.
Condition gradually before striking hard targets to protect the shin.
Stop at any sharp knee, hip, or ankle pain. Informational fundamentals only.
source: General Muay Thai / kickboxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Straight Knee Strike
instructions: From your stance, drive your rear knee up and forward toward an imaginary target around mid-body height.
Point the toes of the kneeing leg down and push your hips forward to add range and power.
Pull your hands or arms down in front of you as if controlling an opponent, keeping your guard otherwise tight.
Return the foot to your stance and reset; practice both sides.
modifications: Practice the knee drive slowly to find your balance.
Hold a wall or chair while learning.
cautions: Warm up hips and hip flexors first.
Keep your supporting knee soft and balanced to avoid strain.
Avoid over-extending the lower back when pushing the hips forward.
Stop if you feel pain.
source: General Muay Thai fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Leg Check / Block
instructions: From your stance, lift the knee of the lead leg and turn your shin slightly outward to check an incoming low kick.
Keep your hands up and your core tight so you stay balanced on one leg.
Meet the imaginary kick with your shin rather than the soft inside of your leg.
Lower the leg back to your stance and reset; practice both sides.
modifications: Hold a wall or chair for balance while learning.
Practice the lift slowly to build single-leg balance.
cautions: Warm up first.
Build shin conditioning gradually; do not bang shins hard early on.
Keep the supporting knee soft; stop if you feel pain.
source: General Muay Thai fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Front Snap Kick (Kickboxing)
instructions: From your stance, lift the knee of the kicking leg up to chamber.
Snap the lower leg out toward the target, striking with the ball of the foot, then quickly snap it back to the chamber.
Keep your hands up and your supporting knee slightly bent for balance.
Return to your stance and reset; practice both legs.
modifications: Kick low while learning.
Hold support for balance at first.
Practice the chamber-snap-rechamber slowly.
cautions: Warm up hips and hamstrings.
Do not hyperextend the knee on the snap; keep it controlled.
Strike with the ball of the foot, toes pulled back, to protect the toes.
Stop if you feel pain.
source: General kickboxing fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Closed Guard Position (Concept Drill)
instructions: Lie on your back and bring your knees up, imagining an opponent kneeling between your legs.
Cross your ankles behind their back to form the closed guard, keeping your hips active and your back slightly off the mat.
Practice opening and re-closing the guard, and lifting your hips to feel how the position controls distance.
This is a solo familiarization drill for the position; real guard work needs a partner and instruction.
modifications: Just hold and feel the position if mobility is limited.
Place a cushion between the knees to mimic an opponent.
cautions: Warm up the hips first.
Do not force the hips or knees beyond a comfortable range.
Guard play is best learned with a qualified instructor and a cooperative partner; this solo drill only builds familiarity.
source: General Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu fundamentals; public coaching references. Solo concept drill described in own words.

name: Shrimp / Hip Escape
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat, hands up in a defensive frame.
Turn onto one side, post both feet, push off the floor and drive your hips back and away, sliding your shoulders along the mat.
This moves your hips out to create space, as you would to escape from under an opponent.
Reset flat and repeat to the other side, alternating down the length of the mat.
modifications: Go slowly and focus on driving the hips, not just the legs.
Do fewer reps if the lower back tires.
cautions: Warm up first.
Keep the movement smooth to avoid straining the lower back or neck.
Stop if you feel back pain.
source: General BJJ solo-drill fundamentals; public coaching references (Grapplearts, BJJ solo-drill guides). Described in own words.

name: Bridge (Upa) Drill
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat, close to your hips.
Drive through your feet and shoulders to push your hips straight up toward the ceiling.
To make it grappling-specific, bridge up and over one shoulder as if rolling an opponent off you, turning your head the same direction.
Lower with control and repeat, alternating sides for the over-the-shoulder version.
modifications: Bridge straight up only, without the turn, while learning.
Reduce range if the lower back is uncomfortable.
cautions: Warm up first.
Drive through the legs, not by arching the neck; keep weight off the head and neck.
Stop if you feel neck or back pain.
Distinct from the bodyweight 'glute-bridges' exercise in its over-the-shoulder grappling intent.
source: General BJJ solo-drill fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Technical Stand-Up
instructions: Start seated with one leg bent and foot flat, the other leg extended, and post the same-side hand behind you.
Push off your posted hand and front foot to lift your hips, then swing your extended leg back underneath you.
Plant that foot to come up into a staggered, balanced standing base, keeping your free hand up to protect your face.
Reverse the motion to return to the seated start and repeat; practice both sides.
modifications: Move slowly through each step before linking them.
Practice the hip lift on its own first.
cautions: Warm up first.
Keep your weight balanced over your posted hand to protect the wrist and shoulder.
Stop if you feel wrist, shoulder, or knee pain.
Best learned with a qualified instructor; this is informational fundamentals.
source: General BJJ fundamentals; public coaching references (technical stand-up is a foundational self-defense skill). Described in own words.

name: Mount Escape (Upa Concept Drill)
instructions: Lie on your back and imagine an opponent kneeling on your torso (the mount position).
Trap one of their arms and the same-side foot, then bridge explosively up and over that shoulder.
As you bridge, turn your hips and head the same way to roll the imagined opponent off and end up inside their guard.
Reset and repeat slowly to groove the motion; practice both sides.
modifications: Drill the bridge and the trap separately before combining.
Slow the bridge down to protect the lower back.
cautions: Warm up first.
Drive with the legs, not the neck.
This solo drill only rehearses the motion; the actual escape requires a partner and qualified instruction.
Stop if you feel pain.
source: General BJJ fundamentals (upa / bridge-and-roll mount escape); public coaching references. Solo concept drill described in own words.

name: Side Control Frames (Concept Drill)
instructions: Lie on your back and imagine an opponent pinning you from the side (side control).
Set strong frames: one forearm against the imagined neck/shoulder and the other hand on the hip, elbows tight.
Combine the frames with a shrimp to recover space and bring a knee in between you and the opponent.
Reset and repeat; this is a familiarization drill, not a full escape.
modifications: Practice just the framing position first.
Add the shrimp once framing feels natural.
cautions: Warm up first.
Do not strain the shoulders pressing into imaginary resistance.
Real side-control escapes require a partner and qualified instruction; this only builds the shapes.
Stop if you feel pain.
source: General BJJ fundamentals (framing and hip-escape concepts); public coaching references. Solo concept drill described in own words.

name: Front Stance (Zenkutsu-dachi)
instructions: Step one foot forward into a long stance, roughly two shoulder-widths, with the front knee bent over the ankle.
Keep the rear leg straight with the rear foot planted and angled slightly outward.
Put most of your weight over the front leg, hips square and back upright.
Hold the stance, then switch sides; this is a foundational base for many karate techniques.
modifications: Shorten the stance to reduce knee and hip load.
Hold for shorter periods while building leg strength.
cautions: Keep the front knee tracking over the foot, not collapsing inward, to protect the knee.
Warm up first; stop if you feel knee or hip pain.
source: General karate fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Front Kick (Mae-geri)
instructions: From a standing guard, lift the knee of the kicking leg up to chamber.
Snap the lower leg straight forward, striking with the ball of the foot and pulling the toes back.
Re-chamber the leg by snapping it back, then set the foot down with control.
Keep your hands up and your supporting knee soft; practice both legs.
modifications: Kick to low height while learning.
Hold support for balance.
Practice the chamber and re-chamber slowly.
cautions: Warm up hips and hamstrings.
Do not lock the knee out hard at full extension.
Pull the toes back so you strike with the ball of the foot, protecting the toes.
Stop if you feel pain.
source: General karate fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Rising Block (Age-uke)
instructions: From a guard or stance, bring the blocking forearm up across your body, ending above and in front of your forehead at a slight angle.
The forearm rises to deflect a downward or high incoming strike upward and away.
Pull the opposite hand back to your hip (or keep it in guard) as you block.
Reset and practice both arms.
modifications: Practice the path slowly without speed.
Keep the motion small at first.
cautions: Warm up shoulders and wrists.
Keep the wrist firm to protect it.
Avoid snapping the elbow; stop if you feel joint pain.
source: General karate fundamentals (basic blocks); public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Outside Block (Soto-uke)
instructions: From a guard or stance, swing the blocking forearm from outside inward across your centerline at about chest height.
Make contact with the outer edge of your forearm to deflect an incoming strike to the side.
Pull the opposite hand back to your hip or hold it in guard as you block.
Reset and practice both arms.
modifications: Practice the path slowly.
Reduce the range while learning.
cautions: Warm up first.
Keep the wrist firm and elbow soft to protect the joints.
Stop if you feel pain.
source: General karate fundamentals (basic blocks); public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Reverse Punch (Gyaku-zuki)
instructions: From a front stance, drive off the rear leg and rotate your hips toward the target.
Punch straight forward with the rear hand along your centerline, rotating the fist to finish palm-down.
Pull the opposite hand back to your hip to add rotation and balance (a traditional karate detail).
Re-set and practice both sides, keeping the punch powered by the hips and legs.
modifications: Practice the hip rotation slowly without the punch.
Throw at reduced power while learning.
cautions: Warm up shoulders, hips, and wrists.
Keep the wrist straight and do not hyper-lock the elbow.
Stop if you feel joint pain.
source: General karate fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Basic Kata / Form Practice
instructions: Practice a simple beginner kata (such as a basic taikyoku or heian/pinan form taught by your school) as a set sequence of stances, blocks, and strikes.
Move through the prescribed pattern slowly, focusing on stance, balance, and clean technique rather than speed.
Turn and step deliberately, returning each technique to a strong stance before the next.
Repeat the full sequence several times; the exact form should come from a qualified instructor.
modifications: Walk through just the footwork pattern first.
Practice a few moves at a time before the full form.
cautions: Warm up first.
Mind your knees and hips on the turns and deep stances.
The specific kata and its details should be learned from a qualified instructor; this entry is a generic placeholder for form practice.
source: General karate fundamentals (kata practice concept); public coaching references. Generic form-practice entry described in own words.

name: Taekwondo Walking Stance (Ap-seogi)
instructions: Step one foot forward about one shoulder-width long and one shoulder-width wide, both feet pointing roughly forward.
Bend the front knee slightly and keep the rear leg fairly straight, weight split between the feet.
Keep your back upright and hands in a ready guard.
Hold, then switch sides; this is a stable base for basic taekwondo techniques.
modifications: Shorten the stance to reduce load.
Hold for shorter sets while building strength.
cautions: Keep the front knee tracking over the foot.
Warm up first; stop if you feel knee or hip pain.
source: General taekwondo fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Taekwondo Front Kick (Ap-chagi)
instructions: From a guard, raise the knee of the kicking leg high to chamber.
Snap the lower leg forward and up, striking with the ball of the foot with the toes pulled back.
Snap the leg back to chamber, then set it down under control.
Keep your guard up and supporting knee soft; practice both legs.
modifications: Kick low while learning.
Use a wall or chair for balance.
Drill the chamber and snap slowly.
cautions: Warm up hips and hamstrings well.
Do not lock the knee out forcefully.
Pull the toes back to protect them.
Stop if you feel knee or hip pain.
source: General taekwondo fundamentals; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Taekwondo Low Block (Arae-makgi)
instructions: From a stance, sweep the blocking forearm downward across the front of your body to finish just above the lead thigh.
Make contact with the outer forearm to deflect a low or mid incoming strike downward and away.
Pull the opposite hand back to your hip as you block.
Reset and practice both arms.
modifications: Practice the sweep slowly.
Keep the motion compact while learning.
cautions: Warm up first.
Keep the wrist firm and the elbow slightly soft to protect the joints.
Stop if you feel pain.
source: General taekwondo fundamentals (basic blocks); public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Back Breakfall (Ukemi)
instructions: Start in a low squat or seated on a padded mat with your chin tucked toward your chest.
Roll backward smoothly along your rounded back, keeping your head off the mat the whole time.
As your back contacts the mat, slap down firmly with both arms at about 45 degrees to spread the impact.
Rock back up and repeat; build height only after the low version feels safe.
modifications: Start from a seated position close to the mat.
Go slowly and keep the range small at first.
cautions: Always practice on a proper mat.
Keep the chin tucked the entire time to protect the head and neck.
Build up gradually; do not practice falls on hard floors.
Breakfalls are best learned with a qualified instructor; this is informational fundamentals.
Stop if you feel any neck, back, or wrist pain.
source: General judo fundamentals (ukemi / breakfalls); public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Side Breakfall (Ukemi)
instructions: Stand or crouch on a padded mat; lower yourself toward one side in a controlled, sweeping motion.
Land on the side of your body with the lower leg and hip taking the roll, keeping your head tucked toward the opposite shoulder.
Slap the mat firmly with the same-side arm at about 45 degrees to absorb and spread the impact.
Return to standing and practice both sides.
modifications: Start from a low crouch close to the mat.
Lower slowly while learning the landing.
cautions: Always use a proper mat.
Keep the head tucked to protect the neck.
Do not post a straight, locked arm to break the fall, which risks the wrist and shoulder; slap with a relaxed arm.
Best learned with a qualified instructor; build up gradually and stop if you feel pain.
source: General judo fundamentals (ukemi / breakfalls); public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Hip Throw Entry (Concept Drill)
instructions: Practice only the footwork and hip motion of a basic hip throw, with no partner, as a movement drill.
From a grip-fighting posture, step in, pivot so your back turns toward an imagined opponent, and bend your knees to lower your hips below theirs.
Straighten your legs to feel the lifting action, then rotate to mimic turning the throw, without actually throwing anyone.
Reset and repeat slowly; the real throw requires a partner, mats, and instruction.
modifications: Drill just the pivot and hip-lowering first.
Go slowly to protect balance and the lower back.
cautions: Warm up the back, hips, and knees thoroughly.
Keep your back straight and lift with the legs to protect the spine.
Actual throwing must only be done with a trained partner on mats under qualified supervision; this solo drill rehearses entry mechanics only.
Stop if you feel back or knee pain.
source: General judo fundamentals (o-goshi / hip-throw entry); public coaching references. Solo concept drill described in own words.

name: Stance Switches (Conditioning)
instructions: From your fighting stance, lightly hop or step to switch your lead and rear feet, landing balanced in the mirror stance.
Keep your hands up and stay on the balls of your feet.
Switch back and forth at a steady rhythm to build coordination and footwork conditioning.
Keep the movement small and controlled.
modifications: Step rather than hop to lower impact.
Slow the rhythm down while learning.
cautions: Warm up first.
Keep knees soft to absorb the landings.
Stop if you feel knee or ankle pain.
source: General striking conditioning; public coaching references. Described in own words.

name: Neck Isometrics (Grappler Conditioning)
instructions: Place your palm against your forehead and gently press your head into your hand without letting the head move; hold.
Repeat with the hand on the back of the head, then each side, pressing lightly and holding each direction.
Use only gentle, controlled pressure to build neck stability useful for grappling.
Breathe normally throughout and keep the spine neutral.
modifications: Use very light pressure to start.
Hold for shorter durations.
cautions: Use gentle pressure only; never force the neck.
Avoid entirely if you have any neck injury or condition without medical clearance.
Stop immediately at any pain, dizziness, or tingling.
source: General grappling conditioning (neck isometrics); public coaching references. Described in own words.
